petitioner_background: The petitioner is a performing artist (comedian/singer) from the Philippines seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA. He performed at various Filipino comedy clubs including The Library Sing-Along Bar, and made a one-time appearance on the television show 'World Musikahan' which airs on The Filipino Channel (TFC). He is a member of the Actors Guild of the Philippines and the Katpunan Porvenir Theatre Company. The matter came before the AAO on a motion to reopen after the Nebraska Service Center director denied the petition and the AAO dismissed a prior appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Photographs of trophies and award ceremonies showing a small number of individuals sitting on the floor in a room with a drape and balloons, purporting to show the petitioner won 'Entertainer of the Year' at the 16th and 19th Golden Book Awards in 2001 and 2004, and 'Best Dressed' at the 13th, 14th, 15th, 16th, 17th, and 18th Golden Book Awards in 1998-2003. A July 30, 2004 article in the 'Round the Biz' section of the Manila Bulletin (which contradicted the dates, indicating the Entertainer of the Year awards were received at the 17th and 19th ceremonies in 2002 and 2004). An unsigned and unpublished document addressed to counsel about the Golden Book Awards describing them as a 'coveted plum' but also stating 'There are about twenty two awards that the owner happily gives to everyone every anniversary. Mostly, to uplift the credibility of the hosts in their acts the whole year through.' An April 15, 2002 issue of Time Magazine Asia listing The Library as one of the best entertainment places to visit in Manila, and an article in Intrigue reporting on the awards.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the awards were limited to performers at a single comedy club (The Library) and thus were not national in scope. The photograph of the trophy itself appeared enhanced with regard to the inscription, reducing its evidentiary weight. The petitioner failed to resolve inconsistencies in the record regarding the dates of the awards per Matter of Ho. The 'Best Dressed' awards were not shown to represent recognition for excellence as a performer. The Intrigue article appeared to be a promotional press release (the photos and text were by the same person who authored the unsigned document about the awards) which carries less weight than independent journalistic coverage. The Time Magazine Asia article went to the club's reputation, not whether the awards it issues are nationally or internationally recognized. The AAO noted other Filipino comedy clubs like Klownz and Comic Lab also receive media attention, and their performers could not compete for The Library's awards. An award limited to a pool of performers at a single comedy club, even a distinguished one, cannot constitute a lesser nationally or internationally recognized award.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's membership in the Actors Guild of the Philippines and the Katpunan Porvenir Theatre Company.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: On motion, counsel did not contest the AAO's previous finding that the petitioner had not demonstrated that his memberships in these organizations could meet this criterion. The AAO reaffirmed its prior finding by incorporating the earlier analysis by reference.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Copies of press releases, promotional materials (including posters), and articles appearing on the Internet and in print. Specific articles 'about' the petitioner included: an article in Midweek Balita (where the petitioner appears with counsel, with no journalist byline), an article in FAB, an article in the Manila Bulletin, and an article allegedly from the Philippine Daily Inquirer as reprinted on The Library's website. Internet search results for the petitioner's name (searched without quotes). Information from Wikipedia regarding the circulation of The Manila Bulletin.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO reaffirmed that materials postdating the filing of the petition cannot be considered per Matter of Katigbak and 8 C.F.R. §§ 103.2(b)(1),(12). Promotional materials such as posters do not appear in professional or trade journals or other major media. Internet search results (especially searches without quotes, which return irrelevant results) cannot satisfy the criterion. The petitioner failed to submit certified translations for foreign language materials as required by 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.5(h)(3)(iii) and 103.2(b)(3). International accessibility via the internet is not a realistic indicator of whether a publication qualifies as a major medium. Most articles were not primarily about the petitioner but mentioned his name in lists of performers at events. Midweek Balita was found to be a regional Filipino-American publication available only in Southern California. Wikipedia information about the Manila Bulletin was declined due to reliability concerns (citing Wikipedia's own disclaimers about its open, user-edited content). Counsel's unsupported assertions are not evidence per Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. It was the petitioner's burden, not the AAO's, to research publications. The AAO noted that even if the Manila Bulletin and Philippine Daily Inquirer were accepted as qualifying major media, the evidence would still fall short of establishing that the petitioner meets any other criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence was submitted but not specifically described in the motion decision; counsel did not challenge the prior finding on motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: On motion, counsel did not challenge the AAO's prior decision that the petitioner had not established he meets this criterion. The AAO reaffirmed its findings by incorporating the prior decision by reference.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence was submitted but not specifically described in the motion decision; counsel did not challenge the prior finding on motion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: On motion, counsel did not challenge the AAO's prior decision that the petitioner had not established he meets this criterion. The AAO reaffirmed its findings by incorporating the prior decision by reference.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's appearance on the television show 'World Musikahan' hosted on The Filipino Channel (TFC), and appearances at Naughty Strings Acoustic Comedy Bar, KLOWNZ Bar and Restaurant, Sitcom Live, Library Sing-Along Bar and Restaurant, and Comic Lab Bar & Restaurant. An article allegedly from Business Wire reproduced on Encyclopedia.com about TFC, stating 'TFC is viewed by more than one million Filipinos around the globe every day' and that 'World Musikahan' is one of TFC's most popular programs. An undated press release indicating the petitioner appeared with two other singers on one episode of the show. Counsel asserted that the petitioner served as a 'featured special guest performer' and that an invitation to appear on the show 'is an honor in itself.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: On motion, counsel only addressed the petitioner's one-time appearance on 'World Musikahan,' so the AAO incorporated its prior findings on the comedy club appearances by reference. Even assuming the television show and/or station had a distinguished reputation, the petitioner did not establish that his appearances amounted to a leading or critical role. The show hosts many different types of artists including 'notable Pinoy songwriters and lyricists, up-and-coming Fil-Am entertainers and even a karaoke jam night featuring renowned karaoke club legends.' The record contained no evidence of additional appearances or evidence of how the petitioner's appearance as one of three performers led to the show's success or standing. A one-time appearance on a variety show is not a leading or critical role for that variety show.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO granted the motion to reopen but affirmed its previous decision and denied the petition. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself as a performer to such an extent that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. While the evidence indicated the petitioner showed talent as a performer, it was not persuasive that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field. The petitioner failed to meet the required three criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), and the burden of proof rests solely with the petitioner per section 291 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Obaigbena, 19 I. & N. Dec. 533 (BIA 1988)
Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez, 17 I. & N. Dec. 503 (BIA 1980)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted that a photograph of the 'Entertainer of the Year' trophy appeared to be enhanced with regard to the inscription, reducing its evidentiary weight. There was an unresolved discrepancy regarding when the first 'Entertainer of the Year' award was issued — the petitioner claimed 2001 (16th Golden Book Awards), but a Manila Bulletin article indicated it was at the 17th ceremony in 2002. The petitioner failed to resolve these inconsistencies with independent objective evidence per Matter of Ho. Additionally, an article in Intrigue reporting on the awards appeared to be a promotional press release rather than independent journalism, as the photos and text were authored by the same individual who prepared an unsigned document submitted by the petitioner about the Golden Book Awards. The petitioner also failed to provide certified translations for foreign language materials as required by regulation.
